interactions.teaches: Hovering over different parts of the figure will display the specific label descriptions, aiding in understanding the data flow and operations applied at each step.
Clicking on a specific feature map layer will highlight its connections to the next layer, clarifying how data progresses through the layers.

demo_steps.title: Understanding Layer 1
demo_steps.narration: Here you see the input data represented as a cuboid entering the system. Layer 1 processes the input using several filters to produce its feature maps, represented collectively as pyramid shapes. Notice the transformation as data is condensed into specific features.

demo_steps.title: Progressing to Layer 2
demo_steps.narration: Now see how Layer 1's feature maps feed into Layer 2. Each pyramid layer leads to new feature maps, continuing data refinement. Connections here demonstrate how each output from Layer 1 feeds into specific areas of Layer 2, emphasizing data flow.

demo_steps.title: Tracing Data Connections
demo_steps.narration: Click on any feature map in Layer 1 to trace its path to Layer 2's corresponding elements. This exercise highlights the cross-connections and broadens the understanding of data interactions between layers.

plan.camera_suggestion: Front-facing orthographic view with an isometric perspective that encompasses both layers and emphasizes data connections between them. The camera should be tilted slightly to provide a spatial understanding of depth without losing visibility of labels.

inline_constraints.framing: match the source crop and whitespace; do not force-fill the iframe if the original has margins

plan.notes: Render pyramid layers preserving relative sizes and depths for clarity. Connection lines should be using a semi-transparent style to avoid obscuring feature maps. Ensure labels are legible by default, with additional details provided dynamically via interactions.

plan.contextChunk:
:::

One way of avoiding this would be to introduce these types of images in
the training set and to repeat the whole process.

## Feature Maps in CNNs

One of the most important concepts when working with CNNs is the feature
map. A feature map can be a channel of the output of a conv layer (as we
defined above) or it can refer to the entire stack of channels at some
layer of a network. The idea is that these are *features* of the input
data and the features are arranged in a *map* -- an array that matches
the shape of the input data. For images, feature maps are 2D spatial
arrays, for videos they are 3D space-time arrays, and so forth.

@fig-convolutional_neural_nets-feature_maps_schematic shows the
interplay between feature maps and filter banks in a CNN:

![The interplay between feature maps and filters banks in a CNN. You can think of the input image itself as an R, G, B feature map and the output logits as a 1x1 resolution feature map with class logits as the channels.](figures/convolutional_neural_nets/feature_maps_schematic.png){width="100%" #fig-convolutional_neural_nets-feature_maps_schematic}

The input to the network is an image and the output is a vector of
logits. We can actually think of these inputs and outputs as feature
maps as well: the input is just a feature map with red, green, and blue
channels and the output is a 1x1 resolution feature map with class
logits as the channels.

Now let's look at the feature maps in a real network,
AlexNet @krizhevsky2012imagenet.
@fig-convolutional_neural_nets-alexnet_feature_maps shows what these
look like after the first and second convolutional layer of the network:

![A selection of feature maps and filters in AlexNet. The layer 1 filter with the orange dashed border slides over the input image and produces the layer 1 feature map with the orange dashed border. The layer 2 filter with the green dashed border slides over the layer 1 feature maps and produces the layer 2 feature map with the green dashed border.](figures/convolutional_neural_nets/alexnet_feature_maps.png){width="100%" #fig-convolutional_neural_nets-alexnet_feature_maps}

There are a few things to notice in this figure. First, the spatial

interactions.label: Show filters
interactions.teaches: Toggles visibility of filter banks to illustrate how they interact with feature maps at each layer of the CNN

interactions.label: Toggle Layers
interactions.teaches: Switches between viewing the feature maps at different layers in the network to demonstrate how features evolve through the network.

demo_steps.title: Initial Feature Map Extraction
demo_steps.narration: Observe how the input image passes through the first set of filters, producing the initial set of layer 1 feature maps. These maps capture fundamental edges and textures.

demo_steps.title: Deeper Feature Representation
demo_steps.narration: Toggle to layer 2 to see a more complex representation. Here, the feature maps reflect more abstract patterns and structures derived from layer 1 feature maps.

demo_steps.title: Output Logits Feature Map
demo_steps.narration: The final feature maps aggregate all learned features to predict class probabilities. This is depicted as a condensed 1x1 resolution feature map, showcasing the model's ability to synthesize complex patterns.

plan.camera_suggestion: Perspective view showing the input image at the left progressing through layers to the output on the right, capturing the transformations at each stage with a linear visual flow

plan.notes: Use semi-transparent filters and effective alpha blending for layer visualization. Responsive transitions between layer views should be smooth to preserve the continuity of understanding
